videoTitle: 7 Times Trinitarians Changed The Bible For The Trinity
thesis: Trinitarian scribes, translators, and copyists systematically altered biblical manuscripts to support the doctrine of the Trinity. Seven textual variants are presented as evidence.
steelman: The strongest version of this argument is that textual variants do exist in Christologically significant passages, and that scribal tendencies toward theological harmonization are well-documented in the scholarly literature (Ehrman, The Orthodox Corruption of Scripture, 1993). Several of the seven examples cited — particularly 1 Timothy 3:16 and the Johannine Comma — are genuine text-critical problems where Trinitarian influence on transmission is plausible and widely discussed by scholars.

falseClaims.evidence: Codex Vaticanus does not contain 1 Timothy at all. The manuscript ends at Hebrews 9:14 and is missing the Pastoral Epistles entirely.
falseClaims.settle: Any critical apparatus or manuscript catalog listing the contents of Codex Vaticanus.

falseClaims.claim: KJV essentially alone reads 'God' in 1 Timothy 3:16
falseClaims.evidence: The NKJV and Geneva Bible also read 'God' in this verse. The claim that the KJV is essentially alone is factually incorrect.
falseClaims.settle: Comparison of English translations at 1 Timothy 3:16.

falseClaims.claim: Clement of Alexandria quoted John 1:18 as 'son'
falseClaims.evidence: Clement's extant quotations of John 1:18 use 'theos' (God), not 'huios' (son). The claim inverts the patristic evidence.
falseClaims.settle: Clement's actual quotations in the Stromata and other extant works.

sources.label: Clement of Alexandria — works and theology

falseClaims.claim: 'Theos' in John 1:18 is a Trinitarian corruption of the original 'huios'
falseClaims.evidence: The NA28 critical text reads 'monogenes theos' (unique God), not 'monogenes huios' (unique Son). The earliest and best manuscripts (P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) support 'theos.' Calling this a 'corruption' inverts the current scholarly consensus.
falseClaims.settle: The NA28/UBS5 critical apparatus for John 1:18.

falseClaims.claim: 'Many scholars believe' that 'Jesus' in Jude 5 is a Trinitarian alteration
falseClaims.evidence: The scholarly trend is actually moving toward accepting 'Jesus' as the harder and more likely original reading. The NA28 prints 'Jesus' as the main text. Calling this a Trinitarian alteration inverts the direction of the textual evidence.
falseClaims.settle: The NA28 critical apparatus for Jude 5.

falseClaims.claim: Late manuscripts of Titus 3:6 add 'God' to create a Trinitarian reading
falseClaims.evidence: This variant is not attested in standard critical apparatuses. The claim is unverifiable from the NA28 or UBS5.
falseClaims.settle: The NA28/UBS5 critical apparatus for Titus 3:6.

falseClaims.claim: Hebrews 13:20 has a variant reading 'our God Jesus'
falseClaims.evidence: This variant is not attested in standard critical apparatuses. The claim is unverifiable from the NA28 or UBS5.
falseClaims.settle: The NA28/UBS5 critical apparatus for Hebrews 13:20.

falseClaims.claim: Codex Alexandrinus reads 'body of God' in John 19:40
falseClaims.evidence: This variant is not attested in standard critical apparatuses for John 19:40. The claim is unverifiable.
falseClaims.settle: The NA28/UBS5 critical apparatus for John 19:40 and the contents of Codex Alexandrinus.

falseClaims.claim: The Johannine Comma appears only in the 'latest' Latin manuscripts
falseClaims.evidence: The Comma Johanneum appears in Latin manuscripts from the 5th century onward. Calling these the 'latest' manuscripts is misleading — they are late relative to the earliest Greek manuscripts, but the Comma has a long Latin attestation history.
falseClaims.settle: The textual history of the Johannine Comma.

falseClaims.claim: The KJV Johannine Comma 'replaces' the original text
falseClaims.evidence: The Comma is an addition to the text, not a replacement of it. The original text of 1 John 5:7-8 is preserved in critical editions; the Comma is an interpolation added alongside it, not a substitution.
falseClaims.settle: Comparison of 1 John 5:7-8 in KJV vs. critical text editions.

entries.quote: John 1:18 consensus presented as supporting 'son' when NA28 reads 'theos'
entries.explanation: Inverts the scholarly consensus on John 1:18. The earliest and best manuscripts support 'monogenes theos,' but this is presented as a Trinitarian corruption rather than the likely original reading.

stratagems.name: Forcing a Conclusion by Unverifiable Premises

entries.quote: Claims about Titus 3:6, Hebrews 13:20, and John 19:40 variants
entries.explanation: Three of seven claimed textual variants are unverifiable from standard critical apparatuses. The conclusion of systematic Trinitarian corruption is forced by premises that cannot be checked.
Uses the loaded term 'corruption' to describe what text critics call 'variants' — a normal and well-understood phenomenon in manuscript transmission that does not imply intentional fraud.

entries.quote: Implies all major textual discrepancies surround Trinitarianism
entries.explanation: Extends seven examples into a sweeping claim about systematic alteration. The vast majority of NT textual variants are non-Christological (spelling, word order, harmonization).

scholarlySplits.claim: Whether 'theos' or 'huios' is original in John 1:18
scholarlySplits.nature: NA28 prints 'monogenes theos' but minority of scholars (Ehrman initially, later revised) argue for 'huios.' Majority favors 'theos' based on P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus.

scholarlySplits.claim: Whether 'Jesus' or 'Lord' is original in Jude 5
scholarlySplits.nature: NA28 prints 'Jesus' as harder reading. Some scholars prefer 'Lord' or 'God' as original. Genuine text-critical dispute.

scholarlySplits.claim: Whether 'theos' or 'hos' is original in 1 Timothy 3:16
scholarlySplits.nature: Virtually all modern critical texts read 'hos' (who). 'Theos' (God) is a later Byzantine reading. One of the clearest cases of theological alteration in manuscript tradition.

scholarlySplits.claim: Whether scribal changes to Christologically significant passages were intentional or accidental
scholarlySplits.nature: Ehrman (Orthodox Corruption of Scripture) argues many were intentional; others (Royse, Head) argue most transmission is characterized by carelessness rather than theology.

trueClaims: 1 Timothy 3:16 has a textual variant between 'who' (hos) and 'God' (theos)
Modern critical texts read 'who' rather than 'God' in 1 Timothy 3:16
John 1:18 has a textual variant between 'only-begotten God' and 'only-begotten Son'
P66 and P75 are early papyri that attest to 'theos' in John 1:18
Jude 5 has a textual variant involving 'Jesus,' 'Lord,' and 'God'
The Johannine Comma (1 John 5:7-8) is absent from the earliest Greek manuscripts
The Johannine Comma first appears in Latin tradition
Erasmus initially omitted the Comma from his Greek New Testament
Erasmus later included the Comma under ecclesiastical pressure
Textual variants exist in Christologically significant passages
Bart Ehrman has written about orthodox corruption of scripture
The KJV includes the Johannine Comma
Modern critical editions (NA28/UBS5) omit the Johannine Comma from the main text

youtubeFlags.text: The title '7 Times Trinitarians Changed The Bible' implies intentional, proven alteration. Three of the seven claimed variants are unverifiable from standard critical apparatuses, and others invert scholarly consensus.

The video implies a coordinated conspiracy of Trinitarian scribes systematically altering manuscripts. Mainstream textual criticism recognizes scribal tendencies but does not support a conspiracy thesis.

Appeals to oldest manuscripts when they support the argument (1 Tim 3:16) but argues against oldest manuscripts when they do not (John 1:18). Methodologically inconsistent application of text-critical principles.

weightedAnalysis.interpretation: At 56%, this session is misleading overall. Core claims carry a disproportionate error load — the central thesis depends on inverted consensus (John 1:18), a manuscript that does not contain the cited book (Vaticanus/1 Timothy), and three unverifiable variants.
